sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A university evaluated a semester-long intergroup dialogue program pairing domestic and international students for weekly small-group meetings. Students completed a social-distance scale before and after the program; lower scores indicate less prejudice. Participants in the dialogue program changed from a mean of 24 to 16. A matched comparison group that attended general campus events but no dialogue changed from 23 to 21. Program participants also reported a rise in perspective-taking from 5.1 to 7.4 on a 1–10 scale.
questions.stem: Which group showed the larger decrease in social-distance scores?

choices.A: Students in the intergroup dialogue program
choices.B: Students in the campus-events comparison group
choices.D: Both groups decreased by the same amount

explanations.A: Correct. The dialogue group dropped by 8 points, from 24 to 16, whereas the comparison group dropped by only 2 points, from 23 to 21. The larger decrease is therefore in the dialogue program.
explanations.B: The comparison group improved only modestly.
explanations.C: Both groups changed, especially the dialogue group.
explanations.D: The size of change is clearly not equal across groups.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean social-distance scores before and after an intergroup dialogue program and in a comparison group.

questions.stem: The program’s effect is most consistent with which social-psychological theory?

explanations.D: Correct. The contact hypothesis proposes that structured, meaningful interaction between members of different groups can reduce prejudice. Weekly intergroup dialogue paired with lower social-distance scores fits that framework closely.
explanations.A: Social loafing concerns reduced individual effort in groups, not prejudice reduction through contact.
explanations.B: Yerkes-Dodson concerns arousal and performance, not intergroup attitudes.

questions.stem: Why is the increase in perspective-taking important to the interpretation?

choices.B: It suggests a plausible mechanism by which dialogue could reduce prejudice
choices.A: It proves that every participant became close friends
choices.C: It shows the campus-events group was identical in every respect
choices.D: It makes the social-distance scale unnecessary

explanations.B: Correct. If perspective-taking rose alongside lower social distance, then empathy and seeing the other group’s viewpoint may be part of the pathway through which dialogue changed attitudes.
explanations.A: Perspective-taking is helpful evidence, but it does not prove identical outcomes for every participant.
explanations.C: A change in one group does not prove perfect equivalence between groups.
explanations.D: The social-distance scale remains the main measured outcome of prejudice.

questions.stem: A high school trying to adapt this program should focus most on which ingredient?

choices.C: Sustained, structured interaction across group lines rather than one-time exposure
choices.A: Separating students by background to avoid discomfort
choices.B: Using only anonymous online polls instead of interaction
choices.D: Replacing dialogue with a lecture about definitions alone

explanations.C: Correct. The strength of the intervention is repeated, meaningful contact over the semester. A high school seeking similar effects should preserve that sustained intergroup interaction rather than substitute minimal exposure or passive information only.
explanations.A: Separation would undermine the contact process central to the results.
explanations.B: Anonymous polls do not create the direct interpersonal engagement described in the passage.
explanations.D: A lecture may inform students, but it does not reproduce the structured contact mechanism.

questions.stem: Which limitation remains despite the matched comparison group?

choices.D: Students were not randomly assigned, so selection effects could still influence outcomes
choices.A: The social-distance scale used numbers rather than words
choices.B: The dialogue program lasted a semester
choices.C: Perspective-taking was measured on a 10-point scale

explanations.D: Correct. Matching can reduce some baseline differences, but without random assignment, students who chose dialogue may still differ in motivation or openness. Those factors could contribute to the larger improvement.
explanations.A: Using numbers on a scale is not the central inferential problem.
explanations.B: Program length does not itself create the key limitation.
explanations.C: The scale format does not explain the selection issue.

questions.passage: A social psychology class replicated a line-judgment conformity task. Participants completed the task with either one confederate, three confederates, or three confederates plus a dissenter who gave the correct answer. The percentage of critical trials on which participants conformed was 12%, 37%, and 9%, respectively. Participants in the three-confederate condition also reported the greatest concern about standing out from the group.
questions.stem: In which condition was conformity highest?

explanations.A: Correct. Conformity reached 37% in the three-confederate condition, much higher than 12% with one confederate and 9% when a dissenter was present.
explanations.B: One confederate produced much less conformity than three confederates.
explanations.C: The dissenter condition had the lowest conformity, not the highest.
explanations.D: The percentages differ widely across conditions.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Percent of critical trials on which participants conformed in three line-judgment conditions.

questions.stem: Why did the dissenter likely reduce conformity so strongly?

choices.B: Unanimity was broken, reducing normative pressure to go along with the majority
choices.A: The dissenter made the lines easier to see
choices.C: The dissenter eliminated any need to answer
choices.D: The dissenter increased group size and therefore pressure

explanations.B: Correct. A unanimous majority exerts strong normative influence. Once one person openly disagrees, participants have social support for their own judgment, so the pressure to conform drops sharply.
explanations.A: The task is not described as visually changing in the dissenter condition.
explanations.C: Participants still had to answer the line-judgment task.
explanations.D: The key change is broken unanimity, not increased pressure from group size.

questions.stem: The participants’ concern about standing out most directly reflects which process?

explanations.C: Correct. Worry about standing out indicates pressure to obtain social approval or avoid disapproval. That is precisely normative social influence.
explanations.A: Systematic desensitization is a therapeutic method for anxiety, not a conformity mechanism.
explanations.B: Latent learning concerns learning without reinforcement, not pressure to agree.
explanations.D: The placebo effect involves expectations in treatment settings.

questions.stem: A workplace manager who wants fewer conformity errors in meetings should most likely do what?

choices.A: Encourage at least one person to voice an independent viewpoint
choices.B: Require unanimous public votes before discussion
choices.C: Punish employees who disagree with the first speaker
choices.D: Increase time pressure so people answer faster

explanations.A: Correct. The dissenter condition showed that even one independent voice can sharply reduce conformity. A manager can apply that insight by normalizing dissent and inviting an alternative perspective before a group settles on one answer.
explanations.B: Unanimous public voting would likely increase conformity pressure.
explanations.C: Punishing disagreement would suppress independence and worsen conformity.
explanations.D: Time pressure would not address the unanimity mechanism and may increase reliance on the group.

questions.stem: Which statement is best supported by Figure 2?

choices.D: Both majority size and unanimity influence conformity behavior
choices.A: Only visual difficulty matters in conformity tasks
choices.B: A single dissenter increases conformity above the three-confederate condition
choices.C: Conformity is unaffected by group composition

explanations.D: Correct. Conformity rose from one confederate to three confederates, then dropped sharply when a dissenter broke unanimity. The figure therefore supports effects of both majority size and unanimity.
explanations.A: Visual difficulty was not manipulated in the passage.
explanations.B: The dissenter condition produced much less conformity, not more.
explanations.C: Group composition clearly changed conformity rates.
